analogy: Think of "The Story of Indian Democracy" through the chapter's own opening images: You will realise that the Constitution has the capacity to help people because it is based on the basic norms of social justice. For instance, the Directive Principle on Village Panchayats was moved as an amendment in the Constituent Assembly by K. After forty odd... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
The Story of Indian Democracy is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that you will realise that the Constitution has the capacity to help people because it is based on the basic norms of social justice.

The text explains that for instance, the Directive Principle on Village Panchayats was moved as an amendment in the Constituent Assembly by K.

The text explains that after forty odd years, it became a Constitutional imperative after the 73rd Amendment in 1992.

The text explains that the Constitution is not just a ready referencer of do’s and don’ts for social justice.

The text explains that it has the potential to extend the meaning of social justice.

The text explains that social movements have also aided the courts and authorities to interpret the contents of rights and principles in keeping with the contemporary understanding on social justice.

The text explains that constitutional norms and soCial JustiCe: interpretation to aid soCial JustiCe It is useful to understand that there is a difference between law and justice.

The text explains that law is law because it carries the means to coerce or force obedience.

The text explains that any system of law functions through a hierarchy of authorities.

The text explains that constitution is the basis of all rules and authorities.

The text explains that it is the document that constitutes a nation’s tenets.

The text explains that the Indian Constitution is India’s basic norm.

The text explains that all other laws are made as per the procedures prescribed by the Constitution.

The text explains that these laws are made and implemented by the authorities specified by the Constitution.

The text explains that a hierarchy of courts (which too are authorities created by the Constitution) inerpret the laws when there is a dispute.

The text explains that the Supreme Court is the highest court and the ultimate interpreter of the Constitution.

The text explains that in 1993 the Supreme Court held that Right to Information is a part of and incidental to the Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression under Article 19(1) (a).  The Supreme Court read the Directive Principle of Equal Pay for Equal Work into the Fundamental Rights to Equality under Article 14 and has provided relief to many plantation and agricultural labourers and to others.

The text explains that more importantly, control of local resources is given to the elected local bodies.

The text explains that the resources are further increased by the grants received through the Zilla Panchayat.

The text explains that they have been particularly effective in punishing men who harass women for dowry and perpetrate violence against them. panChayati raJ in tribal areas Kalavati, a Dalit by caste was apprehensive about standing for elections.

The text explains that source: This was recorded by Mahila Samakhya, an NGO working towards Rural Women’s Empowerment.

The text explains that 3.2 Political Parties, Pressure and interest GrouPs in Democratic Politics A look at the newspaper every morning will show you many instances where different groups seek to make their voices heard.

The text explains that or they form pressure groups who lobby with the government.

The text explains that box 3.6 Max Weber on Parties Whereas the genuine place of classes is within the economic order, the place of status groups is within the social order…But parties live in a house of power… Party actions are always directed towards a goal which is striven for in a planned manner.

The text explains that some, therefore, argue that the concept of pressure groups underestimate the power that dominant social groups such as class, caste or gender have in society.

The text explains that this does not negate the fact that social movements and pressure groups also continue to play a very important role in a democracy.

The text explains that if not, find out and write a note about them in about 200 words.

The text explains that the 73rd amendment has been monumental in bringing a voice to the people in the villages.

The text explains that write an essay on the ways that the Indian Constitution touches peoples’ everyday life, drawing upon different examples.

The text explains that what is the significance of political parties in a democracy?

The text explains that what is the role of interest groups in a democratic system?

The text explains that questions The Constitution and Social Change 39

The text explains that 2006. ‘Disconnecting Experience: Making World Class Roads in Mumbai’.

The text explains that economic and Political Weekly (August 5th). pp.

The text explains that the Argumentative Indian: Writings on Indian History, Culture and Identity.

The text explains that essays in Sociology Ed. with an introduction by H.H.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 3: The Story of Indian Democracy

schemes.name: Ambedkar
schemes.point: Ambedkar argued that local elites and upper castes were so well entrenched in society that local self- government only meant a continuing exploitation of the downtrodden... (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch3 pg 2)

schemes.name: It
schemes.point: It is compulsory now for local self-government bodies in rural and municipal areas to be elected every five years. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch3 pg 2)

ncert_coverage: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Chapter 3: The Story of Indian Democracy
prelims_traps: Do not treat 3.1 The PanchayaTi Raj and The challenges and 3.2 Political Parties, Pressure and interest as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to The Story of Indian Democracy; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain The Story of Indian Democracy through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 3.1 The PanchayaTi Raj and The challenges and 3.2 Political Parties, Pressure and interest to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
